In a purely behavioral sense (on a superficial level), and perhaps pathological sense, the three subtypes of self-preservation/conservation within the visceral/gut triad (SP8, SP9, SP1) can be correlated in a purely symbolic and conceptual way with nearly the entire animal kingdom, because the closest thing we have psychologically to the “most animal,” within Enneagram theory, is the triad whose intelligence operates through the instinctive and the impulsive, plus the obvious instinct of self-preservation/conservation.

The “SP1” aspect of the animal kingdom is found in those animals that possess complex, specific, and seemingly impractical rituals as a way of life for these species, embodying resilience, perfectionism, and a concern for the instinctive fulfillment of certain rules as a means to suppress the anger that these rules generate in our superficial symbolic perception; The best examples of species that fit this description are ants, bees, and some bird species with rituals that include steps making these initially simple rituals more complex—such as building a nest or seeking a mating partner. These are animals that can generally be considered organized.

The “SP9” segment of the animal kingdom consists of species characterized by a general stillness for various reasons. Also found here are the species most likely to coexist with other species “in general” and which, in principle, would have no apparent reason to conflict with those species, either because they are not particularly territorial or because they are species too closely adapted to their environment and lack sufficient adaptability to even attempt to survive outside their territory (such as island species in general, but with a clear emphasis on species that suffer from the same problems as others, such as the famous dodo, which, due to its lack of adaptation to habitat changes caused by humans—such as invasive species or sudden overhunting), the species I believe most embody the “SP9” category of the animal kingdom are domestic animals in general (with exceptions such as certain breeds of dogs or cattle that can clearly be correlated more to SP8, but I won’t get ahead of myself), with an emphasis on farm animals, most of which could be perfectly content with a simple field to live in, the simple and naturally routine lifestyle (even if they are individual specimens in the wild, without adult human intervention) of sheep, pigs, chickens, and some breeds of non-particularly aggressive cattle stereotypically resembles how the sloth is perceived in the SP9 subtype.

And finally; the SP8 side of the animal kingdom, which could basically be the most predominant part and the one that overshadows the rest of the species, but if I had to choose a few species to archetypically embody SP8 within the animal kingdom, it would be—obviously and perhaps boringly—in addition to all highly territorial species, the territorial predatory mammals, especially the large ones; lions, bears, tigers, wolves, big cats, and even smaller species like foxes, wild dogs, ferrets, badgers, and so on, and I don’t think this choice of species for SP8 needs much explanation either; SP8 has already been compared to a bear or a lion (I don’t remember exactly) that only wakes up to seek out and obtain the resources it needs in a conquering manner, as described by Naranjo himself. One could say that SP8s hunt or prey on the resources they want from the world and that, in reality, they are naturally territorial people in one way or another, so I think this point is self-explanatory.

Lack of specification of what one means when using the term 'emotional'. This issue isn’t exclusive to the enneagram, but many users conflate terms like ‘emotional’ with unrelated terms like neurotic, sensitive, and empathetic. These conflations  take words and assign them new meanings depending on the context. These are distinct concepts that are often incorrectly used interchangeably. A lot of individuals tend to assign the traits they associate with neurotic types to unrelated qualities like sensitivity and empathy, as if they naturally go hand in hand.

However, these traits are not inherently linked. For example, a type can be insensitive and unconcerned with the emotions of others while still being neurotic. Conversely, a type can be highly sensitive and empathetic without being neurotic at all. Conflating these terms leads to a distorted understanding of the types, where two types/individuals that are labeled as emotional for opposing reasons get conflated due to the lack of specified vocabulary. There also needs to be a differentiation between vulnerable emotions and neurotic or ‘violent’ emotions. You may have a lack of compassion and love, but you still can have a lot of disgust or anger within you.

title: What enneagram Type is this?

content:
People seem to often misunderstand this type completely. Often seen as a romantic dreamer who is sweet and gentle, many SO4s seem to false-identify as this subtype despite them not being a E5 or understanding the core neurosis of the 5s avarice’s and how this actually presents and manifests in the sexual instinct.

This is a type that can seem very similar to the Sexual six in many ways, often a aggressive disposition of internalized resentment, this usually is not externally presented however, but does not change the fact it exists and is one of the major parts of this subtype.

This is a type that is far removed from its own emotions, is predisposed to be emotionally arid, cold, arrogant, and insensitive to others emotions, as well as a desire to escape the relationship for fear of being devoured by the other. We have a type that can have a counter phobia somewhat seen in the SX6, although presents much differently. 

This type values the primacy and trust of the other, they want the other to trust them fully with a ridiculous standard often that the other cannot realistically meet almost always. When the sexual five becomes disappointed they can turn into anger although mostly presented in leaving them or isolating themselves from the relationship entirely. They often due this out of revenge and anger as they are hypersensitive to rejection. Awareness can come after as a sense of guilt for there cold withdrawal or aggression. 

They can be extremely judgmental of their partner and hypercritical, often though repressed as this type due to its lack of vitality and poverty has difficulty with direct confrontation and can be overly docile at times.
They will often sink in their own thoughts about why they do not have a partner, justifying it and presenting themselves as special and not ordinary enough to get with most “Normal people”

The neurotic solution for this type of the 5s greed is to withdrawal while in the relationship, since greed and the instinct of Eros are at opposites the are at a constant tug of war, this type wants to be invaded emotionally but they also fear it. They often isolate themselves from their own partner while in the relationship, and can have an arrogant or even vengeful attitude of not giving people attention due to their fear of showing their own fragility and extreme sensitivity. 

This 5 does not accept their own fragility, being the most rebellious of the 5s, often showing an image of someone who is strong or who goes against the grain, someone who does not need emotions.

They however prefer to withdrawal rather to act, as the avarice of the 5 gives them a strong sense of inner poverty, they are afraid of losing the little that have.

When this type can get disappointed with their partner they can immediately leave them in some cases or shut them out completely, adopting a cold attitude of indifference.

As it is often disappointed with the reality of relationship’s as opposed to their ideal, they can give up the search for a partner entirely, devoting themselves to the pursuit of art, nature, or a spiritual purpose of god.

SP8 or SO3

Good evening, I have a question. Is it normal to identify more with SP8 than SO3? Even though many people outwardly say you look more like SO3, at the same time I've also said... You look like you have SP8 + SO8, and people have even called you "paranoid." content: hi lol

Let’s get one thing straight — when I say Lust is the umbrella term for the Gut triad, I don’t mean Lust in the shallow, purely physical sense people love to reduce it to; it’s not just about craving or desire. 

Think of it as an intense, restless hunger for life to feel real, immediate, and in your hands. A drive to either take control of what’s in front of you or to resist being controlled by it.

Much like how Naranjo suggested Vanity isn’t just about Type Three but speaks to something deeper running through the entire Heart triad, Lust works the same way for the Gut types (8, 9, and 1). It shows up differently in each type, but the core is the same — a refusal to be passive, a need to react to life, to either challenge it, merge with it, or fix it.

And no — Type Eights aren’t mindless brawlers like people lazily claim. Eights are fully aware of rules, morality, and how the world works. In fact, that awareness is why they’re so good at bending, breaking, or using those rules when it suits them. They’re not thoughtless; they just don’t obsess over every thought like the Head types do. Thinking comes in when it’s useful. Therefore, it’s a weapon, not a shield.

Even Nines and Ones are driven by this same Lust, though it’s hidden under different masks: Nines crave peace not just because it’s nice, but because constant conflict threatens their sense of inner control. Ones chase perfection because the world’s chaos feels like a personal violation of what should be. Lust isn’t always loud — sometimes it’s quiet, stubborn, and obsessive.

But it’s always there, existing as one's intrusive desire.

So, when people look for a word to capture what ties the Gut triad together — that raw, instinctive urge to push back, take hold, or make right — Lust fits better than most. Not the shallow kind, but the existential kind: a hunger to be alive and in charge of your space.
title: Why "Lust" Is the Umbrella Term for the Gut Triad

content:
Typology isn't meant to be taken seriously in such a way that it impacts your life. 

For some, it's an outlet for intellectual exercise thanks to its "no man's land" nature -- there's no real typology authority, no real way to scientifically back up your claims, and no major premises are agreed upon; the community centred around it is like a pseudo scientific community where pseudo (joking :P) intellectuals can freely theorize and, as a figure of speech, talk  out of their asses. Occasionally, the educated and well-read person will appear and bring new insights, before moving on and disappearing like the rest of their kin.

For others, it's a place to socialize and get to know like-minded individuals who have the same "niche" interest (though I don't think typology is as niche as it was back then). They're the backbone of the community. They upvote, comment, and hype up other users. They might dabble in typings and contribute to the theory-making process, but their main interest lies in the social aspect of the community.

Then there's the rare but depressing type of users: the obsessed. Not obsessed with the intellectual nor the social aspect, but with how they feel about their typing. They take their typing seriously, not issuing from accuracy, but whether or not they think it's desirable. It's sad because they don't see typology as simply a tool of classification like it is, but a Bible-esque life story that determines their life (The Big 5 is the only exception). Putting people into boxes is an inherent aspect of typology, and yet this type of user seems to be wholly against it.

content:
Disclaimer for the traumatic nature of awakening: transformation is an arduous path that often involves a traumatic awakening (C. Naranjo, 2024, 1990). It is a  “descent into hell,” where the individual must encounter their own “inner monsters,” greed, and repressed rage (C. Naranjo, 2022; O. Ichazo, 2024). Breaking the “egoic shell” can feel as though the earth is opening up beneath one’s feet, leading to a sense of annihilation that eventually gives way to a spiritual rebirth or metanoia, a deep change of mind (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2022).

Before jumping into the transformation process, one must understand oneself and derive their character structure from the enneagrams of fixations and passions in order to understand which forces and concepts rule their life since early childhood. I have dedicated separate posts to this process, so I will link the relevant procedures here:
- Fixation Point 8: Morality
https://www.personality-database.com/post/11552751?boardID=327782
- Fixation Point 1: Perfection
https://www.personality-database.com/post/11552748?boardID=327782
- Fixation Point 9: Spirituality
https://www.personality-database.com/post/11552745?boardID=327782
- Fixation Point 2: Independence
https://www.personality-database.com/post/11552744?boardID=327782
- Fixation Point 4: Uniqueness
https://www.personality-database.com/post/11552740?boardID=327782
- Fixation Point 3: Acceptance
https://www.personality-database.com/post/11552737?boardID=327782
- Fixation Point 5: Isolation
https://www.personality-database.com/post/11552733?boardID=327782
- Fixation Point 7: Plans
https://www.personality-database.com/post/11552729?boardID=327782
- Fixation Point 6: Adventure
https://www.personality-database.com/post/11552725?boardID=327782

The transformation relies on two primary cornerstones: presence and inquiry (S. Maitri, 2000). One must learn how to be fully present within one’s body, emotions, and thoughts while experientially exploring their inner world (S. Maitri, 2000). This involves developing an awakened perspective known as the “Witness” (or “Mind-only”), which allows the individual to observe their robotic, automatic patterns from a state of innate awareness (O. Ichazo, 2024).

What follows are the three successive methods (protoanalysis): self-observation and insight, confrontation and “holy war”, and contemplation and the “Holy Ideas”. For the first, one must identify their ruling passion and fixation (as mentioned earlier), which is done by examining their past and daily experiences (C. Naranjo, 1990). This makes the individual recognize the mechanical repeating of themselves, an “asleep” state, of which they were previously unaware (O. Ichazo, 2024, 1976). Next, one proceeds to a “holy war” against the ego, where the individual makes conscious efforts to inhibit compulsive personality traits and cultivate the virtue corresponding to their passion (C. Naranjo, 1990). This stage involves Katharsis (thorough purification) and “conscious suffering”, which is a willingness to stay with negative emotions and the pain caused by the ego’s frustration to use it as fuel for change (C. Naranjo, 1994; O. Ichazo, 2024). The final stage is contemplative, geared toward an experiential understanding of the “Holy Ideas” (C. Naranjo, 1990). These are enlightened perspectives on reality that have the power to dissolve the individual's core cognitive error or fixation (C. Naranjo, 1990; S. Maitri, 2005).

But how exactly does one move from passion to virtue? Well, a key indicator of progress is the gradual change in the “texture” of the individual’s soul (S. Maitri, 2005). As one becomes less identified with their personality, one’s passion loses its motivational force, leading to it getting replaced by its corresponding virtue (S. Maitri, 2005). In order to achieve this, one must move from being identified with their physical form and personal history to realizing themselves as pure, limitless consciousness (E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001; S. Maitri, 2005). Additionally, one must move through the “layer of fear” that surrounds one’s psychological deficiencies, which are the “holes” left by lost essential contact (S. Maitri, 2000). This leads one to discover a spaciousness imbued with the very quality of Essence that seemed missing.

For many types (especially the mental and emotional ones), transformation requires reconnecting with the physical body to move stagnant energy and recover “vital essence” (C. Naranjo, 2014, 2021). However, this process also emphasizes embodiment (C. Naranjo, 2025, 2018). This often involves Gestalt therapy techniques, where the individual stays in the “here and now” to integrate their bodily, emotional, and cognitive experiences (C. Naranjo, 2014, 1997). The ultimate goal is a “Theosis” or state of pristine enlightenment, where the individual’s spiritual life is fully integrated with all levels of their existence (O. Ichazo, 2024).

(References can be found in the comments below.)
title: The Transformation Process.

content:
To determine if one is fixated on point 8, one must look for a personality structure driven by the passion of lust and the cognitive fixation on vengeance and rebellion (C. Naranjo, 1990; O. Ichazo, 2024). This character is characterized by the fundamental belief that life is a struggle in which the strong prevail and the weak fail, leading to a compensatory drive to assert control, power, and autonomy (C. Naranjo, 2024; S. Maitri, 2005; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). The primary indicator to look out for is a forceful, commanding presence, often accompanied by a readiness to confront perceived threats, injustice, or weakness in others (C. Naranjo, 1994, 1990; S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001).

This character gives rise to three subtypes, one conservational, the next sexual, and the last social. The conservation subtype is the most “armored” and withdrawn, focusing on material security, territorial control, and physical comfort (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). They often act strategically, like “used car salespeople,” enduring hardship to ensure survival and avoid deprivation (C. Naranjo, 2024; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). The sexual subtype is the most emotionally intense and provocative, seeking fusion and possession of the partner or social scene to escape inner deadness (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000). Their drive for total connection can be mistaken for dominance or control, and seduction is frequently intertwined with intensity and assertiveness (E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). The social subtype is the “social antisocial,” identifying with the oppressed while assuming the role of protector or leader of their group (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000). Their loyalty and complicity are expressed in active resistance to perceived hypocrisy or authority, often through confrontation or leadership (S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001).

Transformation for this character involves a radical shift from lust to innocence and from vengeance to “Holy Truth” (C. Naranjo, 2024; S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). The process begins with recognizing that the “strong” persona is a defensive armor used to protect a tender, frightened inner child (O. Ichazo, 2024; C. Naranjo, 2017). Developing the “Witness” allows one to observe impulsive aggression, dominance, and control without identifying with them (C. Naranjo, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). Integrating the “Soul Child” (Point 2) helps reclaim suppressed tenderness, sensitivity, and a capacity for love (C. Naranjo, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000).

A critical stage involves confronting vulnerability and impotence, learning to surrender control, and accept limitations without fear (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000). Innocence is cultivated through responding to each moment without judgment, memory, or the need for revenge, moving from “truth as weapon” to realization of “Holy Truth”, the multidimensional unity of reality (E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001; S. Maitri, 2000). Therapeutic support is highly recommended, including Gestalt therapy to stay with immediate experience, and bioenergetics or martial arts to release frozen energy in the jaw, chest, and torso (C. Naranjo, 2017). Through these practices, one learns to inhabit softness, receptivity, and presence, ultimately realizing that reality is fundamentally supportive rather than combative, and that true power arises from alignment with Essence, not domination (S. Maitri, 2000; C. Naranjo, 2024).

(References can be found in the comments below.)
title: Fixation Point 8: Morality.

content:
To determine if one is fixated on point 1, one must look for a personality structure driven by the passion of anger and the cognitive fixation on resentment and perfectionism (C. Naranjo, 1990; O. Ichazo, 2024). This character is built on an underlying conviction that they are fundamentally flawed or “not right,” leading to a compensatory drive to be morally superior, organized, and disciplined (C. Naranjo, 2024; S. Maitri, 2005; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). The primary indicator is a constant internal critique, often aimed both at themselves and at the external world, reflecting a belief that perfection is necessary for love, safety, or moral correctness (C. Naranjo, 1994, 1990; S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001).

This character gives rise to three subtypes, one conservational, the next social, and the last sexual. The conservation subtype is the “warmest” one, appearing kind, helpful, and conscientious while channeling anger into anxiety and a compulsive need to anticipate and solve survival-related problems (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). The social subtype expresses anger through rigidity and a “teacher mentality,” maintaining an aristocratic or morally superior stance, often rejecting a world they perceive as imperfect (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000). The sexual subtype is the most extroverted and vehement, directly expressing anger as a reformer, feeling entitled to correct or push others to meet their high standards (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017).

From an external perspective, the most outstanding characteristic of this character is a sense of “angry virtue”, which is a controlled, critical, and demanding demeanor underpinned by moral righteousness and good intentions (C. Naranjo, 2024; S. Maitri, 2000). They often appear formal, disciplined, and intense, employing a “preaching” or instructional style to offer unsolicited advice or correction. Observers may notice a paradoxical tension, like a “Ferrari with the handbrake on,” sensing potent energy restrained by self-control (S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001).

Transformation for this character involves a radical shift from anger to serenity and from resentment to “Holy Perfection” (C. Naranjo, 2024; S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). The process begins by cultivating an awakened “Witness” to observe the relentless inner judge and the belief that one is only lovable if perfect (O. Ichazo, 2024; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). A critical stage involves “letting the river flow,” surrendering the compulsion to control and embracing the inherent rightness of reality as it is (C. Naranjo, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000).

Integration of the “Soul Child” allows for the return of spontaneity, joy, and playfulness repressed in childhood, complementing the disciplined aspects of the personality (C. Naranjo, 2017). Somatic work, including bioenergetics or Gestalt therapy, is recommended to release blocked vital energy, facilitating the movement from intellectualization to direct emotional experience (C. Naranjo, 2017; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001).

(References can be found in the comments below.)
title: Fixation Point 1: Perfection.

content:
To determine if one is fixated on point 9, one must look for a personality structure driven by the passion of sloth (psychospiritual inertia) and the cognitive fixation on indolence and self-forgetting (C. Naranjo, 1990; O. Ichazo, 2024). This character is characterized by a fundamental loss of interiority and a resignation regarding their own sense of “Being” (C. Naranjo, 2024; S. Maitri, 2005; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). The primary indicator is a tendency to adapt to others while neglecting personal needs, resulting in psychic sleep, resistance to change, and self-numbing behaviors (C. Naranjo, 1994, 1990; S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001).

This character gives rise to three subtypes, one conservational, the next sexual, and the last social. The conservation subtype focuses on satisfying physical hunger and creating comforts, using indulgence in food, sleep, or entertainment as a defensive distraction to avoid inner pain or existential emptiness (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). The sexual subtype is oriented toward symbiosis, seeking to fill inner voids by merging completely with another person, often appearing shy or “transparent” in relationships to avoid conflict and maintain peace (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000). The social subtype seemingly overcomes inertia through extraordinary participation and helpfulness in groups, sometimes becoming workaholics as a way to assert belonging and avoid confronting their own presence (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017).

From an external perspective, the most outstanding characteristic of this character is their calm, sweet, and unperturbed demeanor, which often masks a profound insensitivity to their own inner life (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017). Observers may note a “sleepy” or deadened quality in the eyes and a solid, dependable physical presence that can feel immovable. They are often the archetypal “peacemakers,” providing a comfortable atmosphere yet leaving others with a sense of limited real contact, as they tend to deflect into trivialities or habits when confronted with deep emotion or conflict (S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001).

In order to transform, one must embrace a radical shift from sloth to action and from indolence to “Holy Love” (C. Naranjo, 2024; S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). Transformation begins with developing the “Witness,” observing mechanical habits of self-narcotization and self-forgetting without becoming identified with them (O. Ichazo, 2024; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001).

Confronting the “void” is another central stage. Instead of filling emptiness with comforts or others’ presence, they must stay present with fundamental non-being, discovering that this apparent “hole” is a source of immortal being and authentic love (C. Naranjo, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000). A critical stage involves shifting from other-directedness to inner-directedness, reserving time for personal awareness, bodily sensations, and gut-level needs (C. Naranjo, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000). Recovery of healthy anger is essential: by confronting repressed fear and rage, the Nine reconnects with vital power and asserts their right to exist.

Cultivating discrimination is essential, learning to prioritize what is truly necessary for spiritual and personal development rather than getting lost in non-essential details. Therapeutic support is particularly beneficial, including loving but firm confrontation and body-centered practices like Gestalt or theater, which help pierce the “elephant skin” of avoidance and self-forgetting (S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001).

(References can be found in the comments below.)
title: Fixation Point 9: Spirituality.

content:
To determine if one is fixated on point 2, one must look for a personality structure driven by the passion of pride and the cognitive fixation on flattery and false love (C. Naranjo, 1990; O. Ichazo, 2024). This character is characterized by a need to construct a grandiose and exalted self-image, fueled by being loved, admired, and seen as indispensable by others (C. Naranjo, 2024; S. Maitri, 2005; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). The primary indicator is “egocentric generosity,” where outward acts of giving mask a neurotic drive to be indispensable and irreplaceable (C. Naranjo, 1994, 1990; S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001).

This character gives rise to three subtypes, one conservational, the next sexual, and the last social. The conservation subtype appears tender, fragile, and childlike rather than overtly proud. They use apparent helplessness to seduce others into protective roles, believing they deserve special treatment or “privilege” simply for existing (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). The sexual subtype is the quintessential Two, focusing on seduction, charm, and intense magnetism to make a particular person the object of their desire, combining sweetness with assertive demand to capture attention and devotion (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000). The social subtype is ambitious and intellectually oriented, often resembling an Emperor or Empress who seeks recognition and influence within a group or social network, using friendliness or leadership to feed their pride (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017).

From an external perspective, the most outstanding characteristic of this character is radiant, seductive vitality, coupled with a pervasive sense of “abundance” (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017). They often possess a permanent smile and a soft yet commanding presence, radiating warmth and generosity. However, this can be accompanied by blurred boundaries or invasive behaviors, as their giving is frequently transactional or designed to elicit acknowledgment and appreciation. Observers may notice dramatic scenes or fits of pique if their efforts are not recognized or reciprocated (S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001).

In order to transform, one must embrace a radical shift from pride to humility and from flattery to “Holy Will” (C. Naranjo, 2024; S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). Transformation begins with facing the “beggar” within, unmasking the false abundance, and acknowledging the deep inner void and sense of worthlessness that underlie their exaggerated generosity (O. Ichazo, 2024; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001).

A critical stage involves dismantling the mask of helpfulness, recognizing that outward generosity may function as a manipulative tool to secure self-worth, and learning to ask directly for what one needs rather than seducing others into providing it (C. Naranjo, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000). Paradoxically, healing requires shifting from other-directedness to genuine self-centeredness by attending to personal needs and boundaries, slowing frantic activity, and cultivating an authentic sense of self (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017).

Integration of the “Soul Child” is also essential. Behind the sweet façade lies a jealous or petty inner child, whose recognition allows the Two to acknowledge intrinsic value as a “Point of Being” rather than defining self-worth through others’ attention (C. Naranjo, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000). Cultivating true humility involves realistically assessing limitations and accepting “nothing special,” which paradoxically enables genuine freedom and spiritual dignity (S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001).

(References can be found in the comments below.)
title: Fixation Point 2: Independence.

content:
To determine if one is fixated on point 4, one must look for a personality structure driven by the passion of envy and the cognitive fixation on melancholy and false lack (C. Naranjo, 1990; O. Ichazo, 2024). This character is characterized by a pervasive sense of deficiency, possessing a deep-seated conviction that they are fundamentally flawed, inadequate, or “damaged goods” compared to others (C. Naranjo, 2024; S. Maitri, 2005; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). The primary indicator is compulsive comparison, where the individual constantly measures their existence against others, invariably concluding they lack some essential quality or “goodness” that others possess (C. Naranjo, 1994, 1990; S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). This often results in a “paradise lost” mentality, characterized by inconsolable longing for an idealized state of being that always seems just out of reach (C. Naranjo, 2024; S. Maitri, 2000).

This character gives rise to three subtypes, one conservational, the next sexual, and the last social. The conservation subtype is long-suffering and stoic, swallowing their pain and enduring sacrifices to prove their worthiness of love. They exhibit tenacity and a narcissistic independence, refusing to show vulnerability or neediness (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). The sexual subtype turns envy outward in aggressive claim and competition, acting shamelessly to take what they feel has been denied and attempting to diminish or overpower those they envy, using rivalry to compensate for internal feelings of inferiority (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000). The social subtype is the most overtly suffering, characterized by shame, a “begging” attitude, and the tendency to highlight their deficiencies to elicit pity or admiration, often taking on the role of the tragic or misunderstood figure (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000).

In order to transform, one must embrace a radical shift from envy to equanimity and from melancholy to “Holy Origin” (C. Naranjo, 2024; S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). Transformation begins with dismantling the story of suffering, recognizing that the tragic identity is a mechanical construct, and cultivating an internal witness to observe the habit of invalidating what is present in favor of what is lacking (O. Ichazo, 2024; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001).

A critical stage involves moving from specialness to ordinariness, accepting that one is “ordinary” and “nothing special,” which paradoxically opens the path to true dignity. This includes abandoning the elite or unique façade used to compensate for feelings of worthlessness (C. Naranjo, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000). One must also face the “black hole” of inner emptiness directly, learning to stay present in this void without reacting; through this process, the emptiness transforms into a spacious, essential presence, the “Essential Self” (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001).

Healing further involves integrating the “Soul Child”, reclaiming the perfectionistic qualities that underlie their sense of inherent rightness. By stopping self-criticism and integrating this innate sense of completeness, the individual discovers intrinsic perfection and brilliance (C. Naranjo, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000). Cultivating gratitude and equanimity is the culmination of transformation, allowing the person to respond to each moment without being swept away by emotional highs or lows and to develop genuine appreciation for what is present rather than mourning what is missing (S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001).

(References can be found in the comments below.)
title: Fixation Point 4: Uniqueness.

content:
To determine if one is fixated on point 3, one must look for a personality structure driven by the passion of vanity and the cognitive fixation on deceit and self-deception (C. Naranjo, 1990; O. Ichazo, 2024). This character is characterized by a profound orientation toward the eyes of others, substituting authentic experience for a fabricated self-image designed to win approval, admiration, and confirmation of worth (C. Naranjo, 2024; S. Maitri, 2005; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). The primary indicator is the compulsive “ego-go” drive, where self-value is equated with productivity and achievement; to cease producing is to risk perceived non-existence (C. Naranjo, 1994, 1990; S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001).

This character gives rise to three subtypes, one conservational, the next sexual, and the last social. The conservation subtype is the “anti-vanity” one, often appearing understated and focused on efficiency, autonomy, and reliability as a means to ensure material and physical security (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). The sexual subtype emphasizes attractiveness and gendered roles, focusing on being the ideal partner and gaining validation through sex appeal, intimacy, and idealized relational performance (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000). The social subtype is the most overtly vain and “peacock-like,” striving for social rank, prestige, and professional success; they are highly chameleonic, adapting their persona to what is most valued by a particular audience or group (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017).

From an external perspective, the most outstanding characteristic of this character is their chameleonic adaptability and social brilliance (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017). They appear polished, slick, and impeccably mannered, often the “life of the party,” while maintaining a neutral, controlled emotional tone. However, this perfection of form frequently conveys a mask-like quality; their facial expressions may seem superficial, and their eyes can feel distant or reflective of an inner vacuum, revealing the hidden emptiness behind the performed self (S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001).

In order to transform, one must embrace a radical shift from deceit to veracity (truthfulness) and from vanity to “Holy Law” (C. Naranjo, 2024; S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). Transformation begins with dismantling the mask, recognizing that the “successful” image is not the true self, and developing the courage to endure the “loss of face” entailed by not performing for social approval (O. Ichazo, 2024; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001).

A critical stage involves moving from doing to “Being”, learning to stop frantic activity and tolerate silence, solitude, and non-doing, thereby contacting the interior life that has been avoided (C. Naranjo, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000). This brings one to face the void, which is an inner emptiness or fear that surfaces when the carefully crafted social image is dismantled, and requires allowing authentic emotions, including sadness, fear, and shame, to emerge (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001).

Healing also requires integrating the “Soul Child” beneath the efficient adult mask, acknowledging vulnerability and insecurity rather than overriding it with achievement (C. Naranjo, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000). Cultivating authenticity allows one to move toward veracity, embodying a real, spontaneous expression of being rather than a calculated performance for others. The transformed individual operates from a place of inner truth, where actions flow naturally from the essence of self rather than external validation (S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001).

(References can be found in the comments below.)
title: Fixation Point 3: Acceptance.

content:
To determine if one is fixated on point 5, one must look for a personality structure driven by the passion of avarice and the cognitive fixation on detachment and isolation (C. Naranjo, 1990; O. Ichazo, 2024). This character is characterized by a fundamental conclusion often reached in early childhood: that the world is invasive, depleting, or will not provide the necessary love, leading the individual to minimize their desires and decide to “go it alone” (C. Naranjo, 2024; S. Maitri, 2005; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). The primary indicator is a tendency to withdraw into a mental world of information, analysis, and observation, using intellectualization as a protective strategy against perceived intrusion or emotional overwhelm (C. Naranjo, 1994, 1990; S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001).

This character gives rise to three subtypes, one conservational, the next sexual, and the last social. The conservation subtype seeks a “cave” or private refuge to insulate themselves from external demands, valuing solitude and control to save their time and energy (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). The sexual subtype is a “romantic dreamer” searching for absolute trust and a singular, idealized partner, often investing deeply in fantasy and projection to maintain a sense of safety (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000). The social subtype acts as an “expert” or “professor,” accumulating and totemizing extraordinary knowledge to gain social standing, intellectual authority, or recognition while maintaining emotional distance (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017).

In order to transform, one must embrace a radical shift from avarice to nonattachment and from isolation to “Holy Omniscience” (C. Naranjo, 2024; S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). One must first develop an awakened “Witness” to observe patterns of withdrawal and mental compartmentalization without becoming identified with them (O. Ichazo, 2024; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). Transformation involves reconnecting with the physical body and emotions through practices such as bioenergetics, yoga, or massage, which help move stagnant energy and dissolve the “inner desert” of emotional apathy (C. Naranjo, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000).

Subtype-specific work allows one to move from guarding, hoarding, and “taking” to spontaneous giving, loving service, and a recognition of interconnectedness with the whole of reality (C. Naranjo, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000). A critical stage is “descending from the tower,” facing the fundamental sense of deficiency and emptiness directly, and recognizing that this inner void is a gateway to essential presence (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000). For the sexual subtype, transformation requires transmuting “Exclusive Trust” into “Faith in Life”, surrendering attachment to idealized or fantasy partners to fully experience real, grounded relationships (C. Naranjo, 2017; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001).

For somatic and meditative integration, it is essential to inhabit the body and observe emotions directly, breaking through habitual withdrawal (C. Naranjo, 2017). Silence, stillness, and contemplative practices help to cultivate the “internal witness” that can observe mental and emotional patterns without attachment, fostering presence, awareness, and nonpossessive engagement with life (S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001).

When integrating the “Holy Idea” of “Holy Omniscience”, one ultimately realizes that reality is a unified whole in which their inner resources are sufficient, allowing one to shift from isolation and hoarding to generosity, engagement, and loving participation in life (S. Maitri, 2000). By surrendering attachment to control and embracing the richness of interconnected existence, one finds the true freedom of essential presence (C. Naranjo, 2017; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001).

(References can be found in the comments below.)
title: Fixation Point 5: Isolation.

content:
To determine if one is fixated on point 7, one must look for a personality structure driven by the passion of gluttony and the cognitive fixation on charlatanism and planning (C. Naranjo, 1994, 1990, 2019). This character is characterized by an insatiable hunger for “more and better,” an automatic avoidance of pain, and a tendency to substitute imagination for action (1994, 2019). The primary indicator to look out for is vivaciousness or talkativeness, which manifests through compulsive optimism, relentlessness, a longing for variety, and a constant reframing of reality to make it more interesting.

This character gives rise to three subtypes, one conservational, the next sexual, and the last social. The conservation subtype is the most materialistic and “earthly” of the three (C. Naranjo, 2019). They ensure their survival by building “families” or networks of mutual interest, acting as an opportunistic “trickster” who makes deals and finds advantages in every situation (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2019). They may appear rougher or more cynical than the others, displaying a “bad boy/girl” look (C. Naranjo, 2019). The sexual subtype is the most fantastical and “flying” one, often compared to Peter Pan (C. Naranjo, 2019). They live in a “rose-tinted” world of fantasy and idealization, painting reality in pink tones to avoid the desolate present (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2019). They are highly suggestible, enthusiastic, and easily seduced by the exotic or the novel (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2019; S. Maitri, 2000). The social subtype strives to look good and helpful (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2019). They “sacrifice” their gluttony to project an image of holiness, altruism, and disinterest, seeking recognition for being an extraordinary person or a “saint” (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2019). They often act as advisors or ideologists who remain intellectually superior and socially detached C. Naranjo, 2019).

In order to transform, one must embrace a radical shift from gluttony to sobriety and from charlatanism to “Holy Work” (C. Naranjo, 2024; S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). One must first cultivate the virtue of sobriety, which is defined as being fully present in the moment and taking in no more and no less than what is needed (O. Ichazo, 2024; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). Transformation begins when one moves from the “shopping mentality” of sampling experiences to a grounded state where reality is experienced fully, whether pleasurable or painful (C. Naranjo, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000).

A critical aspect of this process is “falling to earth,” which involves moving from the “sky of the intellect” to the “concrete level of the body.” This requires recognizing that real pleasures are often smaller and more ordinary than imagined but are more tangible and fulfilling (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000). One must also confront the “void” and the pain that has been avoided by fantasy, staying with underlying unhappiness or emptiness rather than seeking constant distraction or excitement (C. Naranjo, 2017; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001).

Subtype-specific integration is essential. The conservation subtype must practice disinterested altruism and service without seeking recognition, moving from utilitarian opportunism to a sense of universal human connection (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017). The social subtype must legitimize their real needs rather than disguising them as sacrifices for others, learning to decriminalize self-interest (S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). The sexual subtype must accept the monotony of reality and cultivate commitment, realizing that richness comes from presence rather than constant movement (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017).

For body work and somatic integration, it is important to silence the overactive mind through practices like Gestalt, dance, massage, or other embodied modalities, helping to bring energy back into the body (C. Naranjo, 2017). Silence and stillness are crucial, as one often uses mental activity to avoid oneself, and meditation or contemplative practices support the capacity to contact inner reality (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017). Developing an “internal witness” allows one to observe one’s strategic mind without being trapped in its labyrinths, gradually cultivating presence and acceptance (S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001).

When integrating the “Holy Idea” of “Holy Work”, one ultimately realizes that reality is not a playground of endless desire but a field for meaningful engagement, where sobriety allows one to fully inhabit life’s moments (S. Maitri, 2000). By surrendering the need to constantly pursue novelty or escape pain, one discovers that inner richness and stability are available in the present, grounded in the body and the “here-and-now” of existence (C. Naranjo, 2017; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001).

(References can be found in the comments below.)
title: Fixation Point 7: Plans.

content:
To determine if one is fixated on point 6, one must look for a personality structure driven by the passion of fear and the cognitive fixation on cowardice and accusation (C. Naranjo, 1990; O. Ichazo, 2024). This character is characterized by a constant state of alertness, anxiety, and hyper-vigilance, as they perceive the world as a fundamentally dangerous and unpredictable place (C. Naranjo, 2024; S. Maitri, 2005; E. Jaxon-Bear 2001). The primary indicator to look out for is chronic doubt, which manifests as an inability to be sure of one’s perceptions, leading to hesitation, second-guessing, and an endless search for hidden meanings or “the catch” behind the obvious (C. Naranjo, 1994, 1990; S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001).

This character gives rise to three subtypes, one conservational, the next sexual, and the last social. The conservation subtype appears timid, insecure, and fragile, seeking security through alliances and friendly ingratiation (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). They act like “teddy bears” to disarm potential aggressors and often appear more emotional than the other subtypes (C. Naranjo, 2024; 2017; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). The sexual subtype is often mistaken for an E8. They go against fear through intimidation and a display of physical or mental strength (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000). Their aggressiveness is a defensive “flight forward” to prove they are not afraid, often seeking risk to test their mettle (S. Maitri, 2000; C. Naranjo, 2024; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). The social subtype handles fear by adhering fanatically to rules, ideologies, or systems (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000). They are characterized by intellectual rigidity, a love of precision, and a mind that functions like a legislator to avoid the “fatal error” of ambiguity (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017).

In order to transform, one must embrace a radical shift from fear to courage and from doubt to “Holy Faith” (C. Naranjo, 2024; S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). One must first learn to observe their mechanical internal dialogue and catastrophic fantasies without becoming swept away by them (O. Ichazo, 2024; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). Transformation begins when one recognizes that their “cynical” view of reality is a cognitive distortion created to protect a vulnerable inner child (C. Naranjo, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000). Developing the “Witness” allows one to see how they project their own internal hostility onto the world, creating the very enemies one fears (C. Naranjo, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). 

Real courage is not the absence of fear, but the willingness to face oneself and act in spite of it (S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). One must learn to move from “knowing” to “doing,” as one often uses intellectual analysis as an excuse for paralysis (C. Naranjo, 2024, 1995; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). By taking the risk of making a mistake and realizing it is not fatal, one begins to dissolve the identification with an inferior, helpless self (C. Naranjo, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000).

It is also crucial that one reclaims the authority they have projected onto people, leaders, or dogmas (C. Naranjo, 2017; S. Maitri, 2000). One must confront one’s ambivalence toward authority (the oscillation between submissiveness and rebellion) and learn to trust their own “gut” or natural intelligence (S. Maitri, 2000; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). This leads to the realization that “Essential Will” is the true support, not external security (S. Maitri, 2000).

For body work, it is important to release the “muscular armor” through bioenergetics or martial arts is essential to moving energy that has been frozen in anxiety (C. Naranjo, 2017). Meditation practices like Zazen help contain the fear of “falling into a black hole” or going crazy, teaching the soul to inhabit stillness and silence (C. Naranjo, 2024, 2017). Engaging in “clown work” or theater also helps one to overcome the paralyzing fear of looking ridiculous or being humiliated (C. Naranjo, 2017).

When integrating the “Holy Idea” of “Holy Faith”, one ultimately realizes that reality is not a war zone but an expression of “Being” that is fundamentally supportive (S. Maitri, 2000). One needs to move toward a phenomenological attitude, accepting things exactly as they are without needing to explain or “fix” them to feel safe (C. Naranjo, 2017; E. Jaxon-Bear, 2001). By surrendering the need for control, one finds an unshakable certainty that their inner nature is “Essence”, which provides the only true foundation for existence (S. Maitri, 2000).

(References can be found in the comments below.)
title: Fixation Point 6: Adventure.

content:
Do you base them on consistency between descriptions and underlying mechanisms, or just on surface-level stereotypes? Do you take them from Reddit posts, or simply follow whatever correlations are commonly accepted in the community without questioning them? Or maybe there’s a slight amount of reasoning, but only in service of preserving those old, established correlations? Or is it just “Naranjo said IN = E7,” so it must be true? 

Who knows... everyone seems to be pulling from a different source, and that’s exactly what creates these endless, circular debates.

This is why I think it’s necessary to establish a clear methodological foundation before any discussion. If we don’t agree on the source and framework behind correlations, then the entire argument becomes fundamentally flawed from the start. I was just debating someone who insisted for hours that ET SO1 is valid and that IT SO1 is impossible, repeating the same arguments over and over, but I genuinely couldn’t identify where this idea even comes from. Is it a misunderstanding of what Claudio Naranjo actually wrote in Character and Neurosis? Or is it blind adherence to outdated correlations that people keep repeating without critically examining them?

If we actually share a basis in objective reasoning, linking official descriptions with core conceptual structures rather than relying on vague impressions or behavioral stereotypes, then the picture becomes much clearer. Otherwise, the discussion just turns into people talking past each other. Honestly, I wouldn’t recommend debating someone’s correlations if you don’t understand their source or how they reached their conclusions in the first place, because the entire framework they’re using might be flawed at its core.
title: What exactly is your source for correlations?

Last One.

Short Note: I won't do interpretation in this series. Perhaps it's good to view this work as an alternative to the typical that focuses much on the phenomenological and partly philosophical side of transformation as suggested by Almaas, but take what you want from it.


⓿ 𝗣𝗼𝗶𝗻𝘁 𝗡𝗶𝗻𝗲: 𝗕𝗼𝘂𝗻𝗱𝗹𝗲𝘀𝘀 𝗟𝗼𝘃𝗲


Type Nine is the source of all other types and embodies the primary element inherent in all fixations. This is the element of sleep. What does sleep mean here? It means that Nines are asleep to what reality actually is, as well as being unconscious of who they truly are. They are asleep to the essential in life and give more attention to the external, peripheral, and superficial than to their interior depth. This is true of all fixated types and, hence, this Nine trait pervades all the fixations.


Fixated Nines can be understood as being numb to what they feel, to what is important and significant, which is why they are constantly seeking distraction and busy hoarding and collecting. Ichazo calls the type Ego-Indolence, and it is also known as the Peacemaker. To understand the Nine's ego ideal, we need to look at their other traits and dynamics. 


Maitri describes them as follows:

We will see that these traits of making others comfortable and wanting to be comfortable themselves directly reflect the idealized essential quality. It is no wonder that the Enneagram of idealizations succinctly describes the Nine's ego ideal as "I am comfortable." In fact, all Nine traits-not asserting oneself and not confronting, keeping things harmonious, being good mediators, and so on-are limited reflections of this orientation to comfort and help form the Nine's ego ideal.


It is amazing how the traits of Nines basically result from what is a little twist in their understanding of this essential quality. Most Nines are not far off from this quality, yet this little twist actually alienates them in a deep way from their essential being. We can say that the Nine's traits are an approximation of the apparent characteristics of this quality. But an approximation of characteristics, regardless of how close, cannot connect us with the actual presence. It does not make the mentally constructed personality and belief system become essential being. Nines are, in fact, forgetful of their essential being, heedless about what matters most, and their close approximations simply distance them from the truth and keep them asleep to their true condition, their true being, and to the very fact that they are asleep. Hence their numbness and superficiality. But the ones who wake up to the fact that they are asleep tend to be great seekers of reality and truth. And their realization translates itself into essential or significant action. A good example of this is Paramahansa Yogananda, as we see in his book Autobiography of a Yogi.

content:
“Substitution is the act of putting one thing or person in place of another; the act or process of replacing something with something else, or the thing or person that takes the place of another.” – Merriam-Webster

Substitution may be understood as the act that results from replacing, filling, or appropriating something that is absent, desired, or incomplete. It may also be understood as the act of one who is related to or involved in this replacement, whether as the agent of substitution, the object of it, or as one who intertwines with the process itself. 

The idea of substitution is ttherefore of not only replacing one’s desire to fulfill such kind of emptiness within yet also to have it misunderstood as a desire once it gets replaced.

However, consider that Enneagram IX didn’t really sense something wrong from such a substitution mechanism; thus, treating it as normal, they would rather cling within the fulfillment of such a substitution, whether as a way to feel wholeness, a sense of participation, or more so to fulfill someone’s loneliness, which then resides inside their soul as such kind of a fusion.

If Social 9 substitutes their own sense of desire for the sake of others, Self-Preserving 9 substitutes the desire of others for the sake of their own pleasure, hence then plays an involvement in the act of substitution on itself, then for Sexual 9, it may seem to be both of these, yet it feels relating togetherness in a sense of wholeness throughout becoming the substitute of others.

One may find numerous instances of these Substitution characteristics on Enneagram IX, such as Mahatma Gandhi, a Social 9 figure, who continually substituted his own inclinations and comforts for the collective will of others, dedicating himself to the moral, spiritual, and social upliftment of a nation while quietly harmonizing his personal desires with the needs of millions, not to mention that his acts of fasting, marches, and public campaigns symbolized the ongoing interplay between his inner wholeness and the external demands of the people. He also reflected and lamented when he felt he had fallen short of fulfilling his ideals toward justice and truth, thus internalizing the tension between self and other as a form of lived Substitution.

As for Self-Preserving 9, their character is rather to be different—they would rather substitute the inclinations, energies, or expectations of others for the sake of their own internal balance and sense of wholeness, which still remains deeply related to Substitution. 

And this was noticeable from G.I. Gurdjieff, whose life and teachings, particularly through the Fourth Way, involved orchestrating the intellectual, emotional, and physical centers of his pupils in ways that subtly reframed their actions and desires into his own pursuit of harmony, yet without ever overtly imposing control, such that each lesson, exercise, or group movement became both an instrument for others’ growth and a mirror reflecting his own equilibrium. 

Therefore, he, who could have simply guided or observed, instead immersed himself in the energies of others, drawing their efforts, obedience, confusion, and insight into a complex interplay that simultaneously served his spiritual system and cultivated his own inner attunement, such that every engagement, paradoxical instruction, and orchestrated challenge became a recursive mechanism in which the act of substitution was at once literal, symbolic, and immersive; he substituted others’ impulses to refine himself, yet through that refinement, the boundaries between self and other blurred, creating a dynamic in which Substitution was not merely a strategy or philosophy but a lived, continuous, and harmonically resonant reality.

As for Sexual 9, it may seem to encompass both Social and Self-Preserving tendencies, yet it is primarily distinguished by a deep resonance with relational wholeness and the fusion of self with others’ experiences. One may find numerous instances of these Substitution characteristics on Enneagram IX, such as Claude Debussy, whose music, from the ethereal fluidity of Clair de lune to the immersive textures of La mer, continuously substitutes his own internal moods and desires into sonic landscapes that become both personal expression and collective emotional experience; each note, each harmonic choice, mirrors a subtle negotiation between inner sentiment and external perception, blending self and other until the boundary between composer, audience, and environment becomes harmonically refracted. 

Through this process, Debussy’s art does not merely communicate but embodies Substitution itself—he becomes the medium through which relational wholeness is both expressed and experienced, illustrating how Sexual 9 internalizes, mirrors, and ultimately weaves together the desires of self and other into a continuous, recursive act of symbolic fusion as a whole.
title: On Substitution Theme & Characteristics Of Enneagram IX

content:
So recently (a couple minutes ago while I was showering) I had a thought. Well, its a thought about a pattern of mine.

I noticed this contradictory relationship I have with men. Ever since I was young I found the stereotypical masculinity as primitive, aggressive, almost animalistic, and I always distanced myself from it. That was especially fueled by my relationship with my dad and my parents divorce due to his cheating. I remember very vividly the time when my mom and dad got into an argument for which reason I don't remember. And I remember them getting physical and after some time when my dad was gone from the house for some time, my mom went to me crying and told me that she will miss us and she was gone to neighbors apartment to get away from my dad. I thought I was gonna be alone without my mom forever. Then when my dad came back he was furious and went looking for her. When he found her, I just heard crying from her before the door to our house opened and I saw my dad dragging my mom on the floor. I started hysterically crying at the sight of that. And then he dragged her to their bedroom and threw her on the bed and started slaping her. I was of course terrified and was crying even more hysterically, to which my dads reaction was to yell at me to shut up.

From that day I think I kind of unconsciously told myself that I'll never be like that. And since my dad was always the figure who represented masculinity, I started feeling a deep contempt for it. And that still carries in me. I still avoid as much as possible to not be seen as masculine of any sort. And naturally, I also adopted that same contempt for masculinity towards men. Ever since i was in elementary school I always secretly hated all the boys. They would always make fun of me and ridicule me and make me feel less than. Just to clear the air, I do not hate all men, far from that, I just feel a big contempt for masculinity which most men and boys yearn to adopt.

But the thing is I'm also attracted by that same masculinity. That primal and animalistic nature is attractive to me. And even thought I have a contempt for men, I also unconsciously seek to impress them and be liked by them. Even outside romantic attachments I still seek to be liked by them and turn on my flirtatiousness since that's my main sort of weapon when it comes to seeking to be liked. And I never, and still don't understand why I seek to be loved by the very same thing which traumatized me. I do still have an arrogant attitude towards men in real life.

So yeah, what type does this sound like? Also sorry for it being so long. Lowk im doing this just cuz im curious what type it sounds like
